1: ben-šĕmôneh šānāh yōʾšîyāhû bĕmolkô — ‘Josiah was eight years old when he became king.’ The youngest king to ascend in Judah; only Joash (seven years old, 2 Chronicles 24:1) was younger. The implication is that his reform, beginning in his eighth regnal year (v3), was initiated when he was sixteen — a young man already seeking YHWH independently of court influence. wayyimlōḵ šĕlōšîm wĕʾaḥat šānāh bîrûšālāyim = thirty-one years in Jerusalem.
2: wayyaʿaś hayyāšār bĕʿênê YHWH — ‘He did what was right in the eyes of YHWH.’ wayyēlek bĕdarkê dāwîḏ ʾāḇîw = ‘he walked in the ways of his father David.’ The David-standard (kĕdāwîḏ) is the positive benchmark for Judahite kings. The qualification wĕlōʾ-sār yāmîn ûśĕmōʾl = ‘he did not turn aside to the right or to the left’ is the Deuteronomic obedience formula (Deuteronomy 5:32; 2 Chronicles 17:20; Joshua 1:7) — complete covenantal adherence without partial deviation.
3: ûḇišnat šĕmôneh lĕmolkô ʿôḏennû naʿar hēḥēl lĕḏarôš lĕʾlōhê dāwîḏ ʾāḇîw — ‘In the eighth year of his reign, while he was still a youth, he began to seek the God of his father David.’ The age-structure of Josiah’s reform is unique to Chronicles: the seeking begins at year eight (age 16); the active purge begins at year twelve (age 20); the temple repair and law-discovery at year eighteen (age 26). This progressive narrative shows a multi-decade deepening of covenant commitment, not a single event. lĕḏarôš (to seek) — the Chronicler’s dāraš root, the fundamental seeking-YHWH vocabulary. In his twelfth year he began to cleanse (ṭihēr) Judah and Jerusalem from the high places.
4: wayyĕnattĕṣû lĕpānāyw mizbĕḥôt habbaʿālîm — ‘They tore down the altars of the Baals before him.’ The wayyĕnattĕṣû (Piel: tore down, demolished) describes the physical destruction of cultic infrastructure. The ḥammānîm (incense altars/sun pillars) that were above them (ʾăšer-lĕmaʿlāh mēhem) were cut down (giddaʿ, Piel of gāḏaʿ: to cut down/chop). The Asherah poles, carved idols, and cast images were broken in pieces (šibbar), ground up (hēdaq), and scattered on the graves of those who had sacrificed to them. The comprehensive destruction formula moves from architectural demolition to liturgical defilement of worshipers’ graves.
5: wĕʿaṣmôt kōhănîm śārap ʿal-mizbĕḥôtêhem — ‘He burned the bones of the priests on their altars.’ This deliberate defilement of cult sites by burning human remains on them is the strongest form of desacralization in the OT. The action fulfills the prophecy of the man of God at Bethel (1 Kings 13:2: ‘A son shall be born to the house of David, Josiah by name, and he shall sacrifice upon you the bones of men’). The practice permanently defiled the altars as burial sites (corpse-impurity per Numbers 19:11-22). wayyiṭhar ʾet-yĕhûḏāh wĕʾet-yĕrûšālāyim = he cleansed/purified Judah and Jerusalem.
6: The reform extended beyond Judah’s territory: in the cities of Manasseh, Ephraim, and Simeon, and as far as Naphtali. The geographic expansion of the reform into northern tribal territories is significant: Josiah’s reform is pan-Israelite, not merely Judahite. With the Assyrian Empire weakening (Ashurbanipal died c. 631 BC; the Assyrian system was collapsing by Josiah’s time), Josiah was able to extend territorial influence northward, reclaiming former Israelite territory. The Chronicler presents this as reform activity, not military expansion.
7: wayyĕnattēṣ ʾet-hammizbĕḥôt wĕʾet-hāʾăšērôt — he demolished the altars and Asherah poles, crushed the idols to dust (wayyiddaq ʾad-ʾāḇāq; dāqaq = to pulverize, reduce to fine powder; ʾāḇāq = dust, powder). The same vocabulary is used of Moses grinding the golden calf (Exodus 32:20; Deuteronomy 9:21). The total destruction implies complete cultic annihilation, not just removal. wayyāšāḇ lîrûšālāyim = he returned to Jerusalem after the northern campaign.
8: ûḇišnat šĕmôneh ʿeśreh lĕmolkô — ‘In the eighteenth year of his reign.’ Age twenty-six. Ten years after the purge began, Josiah now turns to the temple itself: lĕṭahēr hāʾāreṣ wĕhabbayit = ‘to cleanse the land and the house.’ He sends Shaphan son of Azaliah, Maaseiah the governor of the city, and Joah son of Joahaz the recorder to repair the house of YHWH his God. The triad of officials (scribe, city governor, recorder) represents the full administrative apparatus. Šāp̄ān (the scribe) is the key figure: he will read the Torah scroll to the king.
9: wayyāḇōʾû ʾel-ḥilqîyāhû hakkōhēn haggāḏôl — ‘They came to Hilkiah the high priest.’ Hilkiah (ḥilqîyāhû = ‘YHWH is my portion’) is the high priest through whom the Torah scroll will be found. The money brought for the temple repair was collected by the Levites who kept the doors (šōmĕrê hassap): from Manasseh, Ephraim, the remnant of Israel, and all Judah and Benjamin, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem — the pan-Israelite collection echoes the northern reform audience of 2 Chronicles 34:6-7.
10: The money was given to the workers (ʿōśê hammĕlāʾḵāh) who were overseeing the work in the house of YHWH. wayyitnûhû ʿal-ʿōśê hammĕlāʾḵāh = they gave it to the workers. The administrative chain from collected donations through temple officials to craftsmen parallels the organization of the tabernacle construction (Exod 35-38) and the first temple repair under Joash (2 Chronicles 24:8-12). The mechanics of temple maintenance are given full attention.
11: wayyi$ĕnû laḥārāšîm ûlaḇōnîm — they gave it to the carpenters (ḥārāš ʿēṣ) and builders (ḇōnîm). Materials purchased: hewn stone (ʾaḇnê māzēb), timber (ʿēṣîm) for frameworks (lĕmaqreh = for beams/frameworks) and for the beams (lĕhaqrôt) for the buildings that the kings of Judah had let fall into ruin (ʾĕšer ḥirrîḇû malkê yĕhûḏāh). The repair work is presented as undoing the damage of faithless predecessors — Manasseh’s fifty-five years included much neglect.
12: wĕhāʾănāšîm ʾōśîm beʾĕmûnāh bammĕlāʾḵāh — ‘The men were working faithfully.’ beʾĕmûnāh (in faithfulness/trustworthiness) modifies the work: it was done with integrity, not corruptly. Over them were appointed Jahath and Obadiah, Levites from the Merarites, and Zechariah and Meshullam from the Kohathites as supervisors (lĕnassēaḥ, the same root as the Psalm superscription lĕmnaṣṣēaḥ: to oversee, direct). All the Levites who were skilled with musical instruments were also appointed over the burden-bearers and directed all who worked in each specific task.
13: The Levitical administrative structure for the repair includes scribes (sōpĕrîm), officers (šōṭĕrîm), and gatekeepers (šōʿărîm). The three-fold Levitical administrative structure mirrors the temple-service organization established by David and Solomon. The Chronicler’s interest in proper Levitical administration of the repair project reflects the theological principle that the sacred structure must be maintained by the properly designated personnel, not outsourced.
14: wayyiṣmāʾ ḥilqîyāhû hakkōhēn ʾet-sēper tôrat YHWH bĕyaḏ-môšeh — ‘Hilkiah the priest found the book of the Torah of YHWH given by Moses.’ māṣāʾ (to find) applies to an unexpected discovery: the scroll was not known to be there. sēper tôrat YHWH bĕyaḏ-môšeh = ‘the book of the Torah of YHWH by the hand of Moses’ — tôrāh here carries the full weight of covenant-constitution, not merely a legal code. bĕyaḏ-môšeh (by Moses’s hand) establishes Mosaic authorship as the scroll’s authority claim. Whether this was the complete Pentateuch, Deuteronomy, or a significant portion is debated; the Deuteronomic parallels throughout the passage suggest Deuteronomy was central to what was found.
15: wayyaʿan ḥilqîyāhû wayyōʾmer ʾel-šāpān hassōpēr sēper hattôrāh māṣāʾtî bĕḇêt YHWH — ‘Hilkiah said to Shaphan the scribe: I have found the book of the Torah in the house of YHWH.’ The first-person perfect māṣāʾtî (I found) marks the discovery as a reportable event requiring immediate official attention. Hilkiah gives (wayyittēn) the book to Shaphan. The handoff from priest to scribe initiates the chain of transmission that will reach the king.
16: wayyāḇēʾ šāpān ʾet-hassēper ʾel-hammelek — Shaphan brought the book to the king. First, he reports on the temple repair: the money has been turned over to the workers. Then: wayyiqrāʾ ḇô šāpān lipnê hammelek = Shaphan read in it before the king. The sequence (report on repair → present book → read from it) shows the discovery embedded in a routine administrative report, making its impact all the more dramatic — the sacred scroll surfacing in a bureaucratic briefing.
17: wayyittiḵû ʾet-hakkesep hanimsāʾ bĕḇêt YHWH — the money found in the house of YHWH has been turned over. The financial report precedes the scroll report. The juxtaposition is typical Chronicler narrative technique: the material transaction (money, repair, craftsmen) frames the spiritual discovery (Torah scroll). The administrative credibility of the temple repair operation lends credibility to the setting in which the scroll is found.
18: wayyaggēḏ šāpān hassōpēr lammelek lēmōr sēper nātan-lî ḥilqîyāhû hakkōhēn — ‘Shaphan the scribe informed the king, saying: Hilkiah the priest has given me a book.’ The naming of the book (‘a book’) rather than specifying the Torah immediately creates narrative suspense. wayyiqrāʾ-ḇô šāpān lipnê hammelek = ‘and Shaphan read in it before the king.’ The oral reading aloud (public recitation of the Torah) is the standard form of covenant proclamation (cf. Deuteronomy 31:11; Nehemiah 8:3; 2 Chronicles 17:9).
19: wayyĕhî kišmōaʿ hammelek ʾet-diḇrê hattôrāh wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bĕgāḏāyw — ‘When the king heard the words of the Torah, he tore his garments.’ qāraʿ bĕgāḏîm (tearing garments) is the classic grief-and-repentance gesture: Jacob at the report of Joseph’s death (Genesis 37:34), Joshua and Caleb at the bad report (Numbers 14:6), David at Abner’s death (2 Samuel 3:31), and Hezekiah at Sennacherib’s letter (2 Kings 19:1). The tearing is not merely symbolic despair but a physical enactment of covenant alarm: the king who hears the Torah and sees that it has not been kept tears himself as his people should have been torn by judgment.
20: Josiah commands Hilkiah and certain officials to inquire (diršû ʾet-YHWH bᵃʿaḏî = seek YHWH on my behalf) about the words of the book. The verb dāraš (to seek, inquire) returns in the royal command: the same seeking vocabulary that initiated Josiah’s reform (v3, v8) now governs the response to the scroll. ‘Concerning my behalf and the remnant in Israel and Judah’ (ûḇaʿaḏ šĕʾērît bĕyiśrāʾēl ûḇîhûḏāh) — the pan-Israelite scope of the inquiry reflects the pan-Israelite scope of the reform.
21: ḥămat YHWH ʾăšer-nittĕkāh ḇānû gĕḏôlāh ʾāšer lōʾ-šāmĕrû ʾăḇôtênû ʾet-dĕḇar YHWH — ‘The wrath of YHWH poured out on us is great, because our fathers did not keep the word of YHWH.’ nātak (Niphal: poured out) is the liquid-outpouring metaphor for divine wrath (Jeremiah 42:18; Lamentations 2:4; 4:11 use the same idiom). The cause: lōʾ-šāmĕrû ʾet-dĕḇar YHWH (they did not keep the word of YHWH) and lāʿăśôt kĕkol-hakkātûḇ ʿal-hassēper hazzeh (to do according to all that is written in this book). The scroll establishes the standard against which the generations are measured; the recognition of failure is the beginning of the seeking.
22: wayyēleḵ ḥilqîyāhû wayyirbê hammelek — Hilkiah and the officials went to Huldah the prophetess (ḥuldāh hannĕḇîʾāh). nĕḇîʾāh (feminine: prophetess) is the same designation as Miriam (Exodus 15:20), Deborah (Judges 4:4), and Isaiah’s wife (Isaiah 8:3). She lived in Jerusalem in the Second Quarter (wĕhîʾ yôšeḇet bîrûšālāyim bammišneh). mišneh (second, Mishneh) = a district of Jerusalem, possibly the newer expansion of the city westward (Zephaniah 1:10 also references the Mishneh as a distinct urban area). That Josiah’s officials consulted a woman prophet — when Jeremiah and Zephaniah were also active — indicates Huldah’s recognized authority was not incidental.
23: wattōʾmer lāhem kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — ‘Thus says YHWH God of Israel.’ Huldah speaks the full prophetic messenger formula. The oracle is in two parts: judgment (vv24-25) and Josiah’s reprieve (vv26-28). The two-part structure matches the classic prophetic oracle of doom with a secondary word of comfort to the inquirer.
24: hinnî mēḇîʾ rāʿāh ʿal-hammāqôm hazzeh — ‘Behold, I am bringing evil upon this place.’ The hinnî (behold me / I am about to) + Hiphil participle is the prophetic imminence formula: judgment is not future possibility but imminent divine action. ʾet kol-hāʾālôt hakkĕtûḇôt ʿal-hassēper ʾăšer qārĕʾû = ‘all the curses written in the book that they read before the king.’ The Torah scroll becomes the forensic document: its curses (ʾālôt) activate against the people who have violated it.
25: taḥat ʾăšer ʿăzāḇûnî wayyĕqaṭṭĕrû lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — ‘Because they have forsaken me and burned incense to other gods.’ ʿāzaḇ (to forsake) returns as the primary apostasy verb. wattikah ḥamatî bammāqôm hazzeh wĕlōʾ tiḵḇeh = ‘my wrath is poured/kindled against this place and will not be quenched.’ tāḵāh (Niphal: to be kindled, poured) + lōʾ tiḵḇeh (will not be quenched/extinguished) form the unquenchable-fire idiom for divine wrath (Jeremiah 7:20; 2 Chronicles 17:27; Matthew 3:12 — ‘unquenchable fire’).
26: wĕʾel-melek yĕhûḏāh haššōlēaḥ ʾetḵem — ‘And to the king of Judah who sent you.’ The second part of the oracle is addressed specifically to Josiah via the delegation. The formula distinguishes between the general judgment (vv24-25) and the personal word to the king (vv27-28). Huldah identifies Josiah by his actions: diḇrê haSSēper ʾăšer šāmaʿtā = the words of the scroll that he heard.
27: yaʿan rāk lĕḇāḇĕḵā wattikkānēʿ mippānay — ‘Because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before me.’ rāḵ (soft, tender) applied to the heart (lēḇ) = sensitivity, receptiveness, the opposite of hard/obstinate. kānaʿ (Niphal: to humble oneself) appears again — the Chronicler’s decisive repentance word. The double basis for the reprieve: tender heart + active humbling. Josiah’s tearing his garments and weeping are the external evidence of the internal condition. wattibkeh lĕpānay = he wept before YHWH — tears as concrete evidence of heart-tenderness.
28: hinnĕnî ʾōsipĕḵā ʾel-ʾăḇôtêḵā — ‘Behold, I am gathering you to your fathers.’ The hinnî + Hiphil participle of ʾāsap (to gather): YHWH promises Josiah a peaceful death before the judgment falls. wĕniʾsaptā ʾel-qiḇrôtêḵā bĕšālôm = ‘you will be gathered to your tomb in peace.’ The divine word of individual consolation within a word of corporate judgment is a consistent prophetic pattern (Jeremiah 39:16-18 for Ebed-melech; Jer 45 for Baruch). The word ‘in peace’ (bĕšālôm) is ironically tested: Josiah dies in battle at Megiddo (2 Chronicles 35:20-24), which the Chronicler will explain as his own disobedience.
29: wayyišlaḥ hammelek wayyeʾĕsōp ʾet kol-ziqnê yĕhûḏāh wîrûšālāyim — ‘The king sent and gathered all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.’ The covenant-assembly after the prophetic oracle mirrors the pattern of David’s assembly (1 Chr 28-29) and Solomon’s temple dedication (2 Chr 5-7). The public covenant assembly is the proper response to the Torah’s discovery: what is found is meant to be publicly proclaimed and responded to communally.
30: wayyaʿal hammelek bêt-YHWH — ‘The king went up to the house of YHWH.’ With all the men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the Levites, and all the people from great to small (haggāḏôl wĕhaqqāṭān). wayyiqrāʾ bĕʾoznêhem ʾet kol-diḇrê sēper habbĕrît = ‘he read in their hearing all the words of the book of the covenant.’ The public Torah reading (qārāʾ bĕʾoznêhem, reading in the ears/hearing of the people) is the formal covenant proclamation of Deuteronomy 31:11 and Nehemiah 8:3. The scroll is now identified as sēper habbĕrît (the book of the covenant), linking it to the Sinai covenant document (Exodus 24:7).
31: wayyaʿămod hammelek ʾal-ʿammûḏô — ‘The king stood at his pillar (ʿammûḏ).’ The royal pillar/column was the designated standing place for the king in the temple (2 Kings 11:14; 23:3 parallel). Solomon stood before the altar (wayya ʿămod lipnê mizbaḥ YHWH, 2 Chronicles 6:12), but the king’s standing at his pillar is the formal covenantal posture. wayyikrot ʾet-habberît lipnê YHWH = ‘he made (cut) the covenant before YHWH.’ kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) is the standard covenant-making idiom throughout the OT. The elements of the covenant: to walk after YHWH, to keep his commands, testimonies, and statutes, with all heart and soul, to perform the words of the covenant written in this book.
32: wayyaʿămēḏ ʾet kol-hanimsāʾ bîrûšālāyim ûḇinyāmîn — ‘He made all who were present in Jerusalem and Benjamin stand [in the covenant].’ wayyaʿămēḏ (Hiphil of ʿāmaḏ: to cause to stand) applied to covenant standing: he constituted the assembly as covenant participants. The standing is both physical (they stood) and covenantal (they took their stand). wayyaʿăśû yôšĕḇê yĕrûšālāyim kabbĕrît ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtêhem = ‘the inhabitants of Jerusalem acted according to the covenant of God of their fathers.’
33: wayyāsar yôʾšîyāhû ʾet kol-hattôʿēḇôt mikkol-hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer liḇnê yiśrāʾēl — ‘Josiah removed all the abominations (tôʿēḇôt) from all the territories belonging to the people of Israel.’ The pan-Israelite scope again: the removal extended across all former Israelite territory. wayyaʿăḇēḏ kol-hanimsāʾ bĕyiśrāʾēl laʿăḇōḏ ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem = ‘he made all who were present in Israel serve YHWH their God.’ kol-yāmāyw lōʾ sārû mēʾaḥărê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl = ‘all his days they did not turn from following YHWH God of Israel.’ The Chronicler’s closing assessment of Josiah’s reform: complete during the king’s lifetime, pan-Israelite in scope, covenant-constituting in character.
